definition: A hierarchical system of land ownership and duties to protect and be protected, defining European society from the 9th to 15th centuries.
A period of renewed interest in classical art, science, and humanism that transformed European culture between the 14th and 17th centuries.
An ancient network of trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean, facilitating commerce and cultural exchange for over a millennium.
The practice of acquiring political control over other countries, settling them with colonists, and exploiting them economically.
The transition to new manufacturing processes in Britain, then spreading worldwide, transforming agrarian societies into industrial ones.
A policy of extending a country's power and influence through colonisation, military force, or economic dominance.
A 1215 charter issued in England limiting royal power and establishing the principle that everyone is subject to the law.
A series of religious wars sanctioned by the Latin Church from 1095 to 1291, aiming to recover the Holy Land from Muslim rule.
An intellectual movement emphasising reason, science, and individual rights that shaped modern democracy and philosophy.
The division of a territory into two or more separate political entities, often with profound humanitarian consequences.
A political ideology that holds that a nation should be congruent with a state, driving independence movements worldwide.
A state of geopolitical tension between the United States and Soviet Union, characterised by proxy wars, arms races, and ideological rivalry.
The process by which colonies gained independence from European powers, reshaping the global political map after World War II.
A system of institutionalised racial segregation enforced in South Africa from 1948 to 1994.
A system of government that seeks to subordinate all aspects of individual life to the authority of the state.
Leadership or dominance, especially of one country or social group over others.
The dispersion or spread of people from their original homeland, often used to describe the Jewish exile and later African and South Asian migrations.
The right to vote in political elections, gained by women in many countries between 1893 and 1971 after sustained campaigns.
A commission from the League of Nations authorising a member nation to administer a territory.
An economic theory that trade generates wealth and is stimulated by the accumulation of profitable balances, dominant in early European empires.
A political theory holding that all power should be vested in one ruler or other authority.
A form of government in which a deity or deity's laws are the supreme civil ruler, as seen in ancient Egypt and modern Iran.
A small group of people having control of a country, organisation, or institution.
The 16th-century religious and political upheaval that fractured the Catholic Church, giving rise to Protestantism.
A member of the ancient Roman aristocracy, contrasted with the plebeians who represented common citizens.
A member of the general citizenry of Rome, as opposed to the patrician class, who gradually won political rights through the Conflict of the Orders.
A member of a people or tribe that has no permanent abode but moves about seasonally in search of food and water.
Relating to cultivated land or the cultivation of land; agrarian societies formed the basis of most early civilisations.
A line of hereditary rulers of a country, particularly used in Chinese, Egyptian, and other ancient histories.
An extensive group of states or countries under a single supreme authority, such as the British, Roman, or Mongol empires.
A state in which supreme power is held by the people and their elected representatives, with an elected or nominated president rather than a monarch.
The approximately 200-year period of relative peace and stability across the Roman Empire, from Augustus to Marcus Aurelius.
The earliest known writing system, developed by Sumerians around 3000 BCE, consisting of wedge-shaped marks on clay tablets.
The formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt, combining logographic and alphabetic elements on monuments and papyrus.
A type of massive stone structure built in ancient Mesopotamia, having the form of a terraced step pyramid of successively receding stories.
A citadel or complex of ancient Greek temples built on a high hill in the middle of a city, most famously in Athens.
A system of government first developed in Athens around 507 BCE, in which power is vested in the people who exercise it through elected representatives.
Plato's ideal ruler, who combines philosophical knowledge with political power, from his work 'The Republic'.
A form of Islamic government led by a caliph, considered a political and religious successor to the Prophet Muhammad.
In Islamic doctrine, the spiritual struggle within oneself against sin; historically also interpreted as armed struggle in defence of Islam.
The centuries-long series of campaigns by Christian states to recapture territory of the Iberian Peninsula from the Moors, ending in 1492.
The devastating pandemic of bubonic plague that struck Europe in 1347–1351, killing an estimated one-third to one-half of the continent's population.
A major split or division within a religious or political group, most famously the Great Schism of 1054 between Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox Christianity.
A group of institutions within the Catholic Church tasked with combating heresy, known for their harsh interrogation methods.
A medieval association of craftsmen or merchants, formed to protect mutual interests and maintain standards in their trade.
Agricultural labourers bound under the feudal system to work on their lord's estate, with limited freedom of movement.
The medieval knightly system with its religious, moral, and social code emphasising bravery, courtesy, honour, and protection of the weak.
The dominant method of critical thought in Western Christianity, using dialectical reasoning to defend and systematise Catholic doctrine.
A leader in the Spanish conquest of the Americas, Asia, and Africa during the 15th and 16th centuries.
A labour system rewarding conquerors with the labour of particular groups of subject people, central to Spanish exploitation of the Americas.
A system of transatlantic trade in which manufactured goods were shipped to Africa, enslaved Africans transported to the Americas, and raw goods sent back to Europe.
A professional soldier hired to serve in a foreign army, motivated primarily by financial gain rather than loyalty to a cause.
An agreement made by opposing sides in a war to stop fighting for a certain time, most famously signed on November 11, 1918.
Compensation required from a defeated nation for damage or injury done during a war, as imposed on Germany by the Treaty of Versailles.
The policy of making concessions to an aggressive power to avoid conflict, notably Britain's policy toward Nazi Germany in the 1930s.
An intensive military campaign using fast-moving aircraft and armoured ground forces to overwhelm an opponent quickly, pioneered by Germany in 1939.
The systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of six million Jews by the Nazi regime between 1941 and 1945.
The act of fighting against an occupying force, particularly referring to civilian movements against Nazi occupation in Europe.
The American initiative providing economic support to rebuild Western European economies after World War II, strengthening democratic governments.
The easing of hostility or strained relations, especially between the United States and Soviet Union during the 1970s.
The term coined by Winston Churchill to describe the boundary dividing Europe into two separate areas of political influence during the Cold War.
A group of states not formally aligned with or against any major power bloc, founded in 1961 as a third path between American and Soviet spheres.
The policy of maximum openness in the activities of government institutions introduced by Mikhail Gorbachev in 1985.
A political movement for reformation within the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, associated with the policies introduced by Mikhail Gorbachev.
The Polish trade union federation that became a broad anti-communist social movement and played a crucial role in ending Communist rule in Poland.
The non-violent transition of power in Czechoslovakia from Communist rule to a parliamentary republic in 1989.
Literally 'shaking off' in Arabic; used to describe Palestinian uprisings against Israeli occupation in 1987 and 2000.
The official policy of racial segregation enforced by the National Party government in South Africa between 1948 and 1994.
A type of irregular warfare in which small groups of combatants use tactics such as ambushes and sabotage to fight a larger and less-mobile traditional army.
Information, especially of a biased or misleading nature, used to promote a political cause or point of view.
A sudden seizure of power from a government by a small group, often using military force.
The 19th-century doctrine that the expansion of the United States throughout the American continents was both justified and inevitable.
The fact or process of being set free from legal, social, or political restrictions, particularly the freeing of enslaved people.
The period from 1865 to 1877 during which the United States grappled with reintegrating the Southern states after the Civil War.
The era of rapid economic growth in the United States from the 1870s to about 1900, characterised by extreme wealth inequality.
A political philosophy supporting social reform, often linked to the Progressive Era in the United States from 1896 to 1916.
The domestic programs introduced by President Franklin D. Roosevelt between 1933 and 1938 to provide relief, recovery, and reform during the Great Depression.
A severe worldwide economic depression that took place in the 1930s, beginning in the United States after the stock market crash of 1929.
The theory that government spending should increase during economic downturns to stimulate demand, developed by economist John Maynard Keynes.
A form of government in which the state protects and promotes the economic and social well-being of citizens, developed extensively in post-WWII Britain.
The practice of acquiring full or partial political control over another country, occupying it with settlers, and exploiting it economically.
The full right and power of a governing body over itself, without interference from outside sources or bodies.
A formally concluded and ratified agreement between states, governing areas including trade, military alliances, and territorial disputes.
The practice of conducting negotiations between nations, central to international relations since antiquity.
An official ban on trade or other commercial activity with a particular country.
The deliberate killing of a large group of people, especially those of a particular ethnic group or nation, as seen in the Armenian Genocide and the Holocaust.
The mass expulsion or killing of members of an unwanted ethnic or religious group in a society.
A person who has been forced to leave their country in order to escape war, persecution, or natural disaster.
The division of a region or body into smaller and often hostile units, derived from the fragmentation of the Balkans following World War I.
An ideology that holds that a nation should be congruent with a state, often conflicting with multinational empires and colonial powers.
An ideology advocating the solidarity of Africans worldwide and the desire for a union of African nations.
The movement for the re-establishment, and the development and protection of a Jewish nation in what is now Israel.
The active, professed refusal of a citizen to obey certain laws or government demands, as practiced by Mahatma Gandhi and Martin Luther King Jr.
Gandhi's policy of passive resistance and non-violent non-cooperation as a means of resisting British rule in India.
The division of British India in 1947 into two independent nations, India and Pakistan, accompanied by mass migrations and communal violence.
An economic strategy to remove the British Empire from power by boycotting British goods and reviving domestic products.
A 240-mile non-violent protest led by Mahatma Gandhi against British salt taxes in colonial India.
The demand made by Gandhi calling for an end to British rule in India during World War II.
A significant but short-lived phase of the Indian independence movement led by Gandhi, in which Indians withdrew cooperation from British administration.
A regal official who runs a country, colony, city, province, or sub-national state, in the name of and as the representative of the monarch.
A major uprising against the authority of the British East India Company by Indian soldiers (sepoys) in 1857, also known as the First War of Indian Independence.
A form of revenue collection in which zamindars (landlords) collected taxes from peasant farmers and forwarded them to the colonial government.
A land revenue system where the government collected taxes directly from the cultivators or ryots in certain parts of India.
A revenue agreement made by the British East India Company with Bengali zamindars, fixing land tax in perpetuity.
The economic theory articulated by Dadabhai Naoroji that Britain extracted significant wealth from India through trade and governance policies.
Self-governance by a community within a larger empire, particularly associated with Irish and Indian independence movements.
Constitutional reforms that introduced a dual system of government in India, giving Indians more influence in provincial administration.
A controversial British legislation allowing detention without trial in India, which prompted widespread protests led by Gandhi.
The killing of hundreds of unarmed Indian civilians by British colonial forces at Amritsar in 1919, galvanising the independence movement.
A pan-Islamic political protest campaign launched by Muslims in India to influence the British government to protect the Ottoman Caliphate.
The idea that Hindu and Muslim communities in India were two distinct nations with fundamentally different customs and traditions.
An agreement between the Indian National Congress and the All India Muslim League that united them in demanding greater self-governance.
Reforms that introduced separate electorates for Muslims in India, a concession to Muslim political demands that had lasting consequences.
India's oldest political party, founded in 1885, that led the independence movement and has governed India for most of its post-independence history.
A political party founded in Dhaka in 1906 that advocated for the rights of Muslims in British India and played a key role in the creation of Pakistan.
The Communist International, an organisation founded in Moscow in 1919 that advocated world communism and directed communist parties worldwide.
The policy of consolidating individual land and labour into collective farms, implemented brutally under Stalin in the 1930s.
A network of forced labour camps throughout the Soviet Union under Stalin's regime, used to punish political prisoners.
A relatively affluent Russian peasant who was targeted by Stalin's collectivisation campaign and was frequently imprisoned or killed.
A government plan for economic development covering a period of five years, used extensively in the Soviet Union and China.
A sociopolitical movement launched by Mao Zedong to eliminate capitalist and traditional elements from Chinese society.
A military retreat undertaken by the Red Army of the Communist Party of China, covering over 9,000 km to escape Nationalist forces.
Two wars in the mid-19th century involving China and Western powers over China's sovereignty and the lucrative opium trade.
A massive civil war in southern China from 1850 to 1864, waged by the Heavenly Kingdom movement against the Qing dynasty.
The political change in Japan in 1868 that returned power to the Emperor Meiji and began Japan's rapid modernisation.
The government of a shogun in feudal Japan, in which the shogun held supreme military power while the Emperor remained a figurehead.
The military nobility and officer caste of medieval and early-modern Japan, known for their adherence to Bushido warrior code.
The era from 1185 to 1868 when Japan was controlled by rival clans and their warrior-lords, ending with the Meiji Restoration.
An anti-foreign and anti-colonial uprising in China in 1899–1901, directed against Christian missionaries and their Chinese converts.
Areas of a country where a foreign nation claims special rights and economic privileges, used extensively in China and Africa by European powers.
The seven-year period for which Bismarck's military bills were passed, representing a central tool of German military planning in the 1880s.
The alliance between Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Italy formed in 1882, one of the precursor blocs to World War I.
The informal alliance between France, Russia, and Britain formed between 1894 and 1907, opposing the Triple Alliance in World War I.
A policy or ideology of extending a country's rule over foreign nations and peoples by force or by economic and political dominance.
Heir presumptive to the Austro-Hungarian throne whose assassination in Sarajevo in 1914 triggered World War I.
Germany's strategic military plan for a two-front war against France and Russia, calling for a rapid defeat of France through Belgium.
The type of combat in World War I in which opposing troops fight from trenches facing each other, leading to years of static, devastating conflict.
The contested ground between the front lines of opposing armies, particularly associated with the trenches of World War I.
The peace treaty that ended World War I, imposing punishing terms on Germany including reparations, territorial losses, and military restrictions.
The government of Germany from 1919 to 1933, established after WWI and ended by Hitler's rise to power.
An extremely rapid or out of control inflation, famously affecting Germany in 1923 when the price of a loaf of bread reached 200 billion marks.
A far-right, authoritarian nationalist political ideology characterised by dictatorial power, suppression of opposition, and control of society and economy.
The Nazi concept of 'living space', used to justify territorial expansion and ethnic cleansing in Eastern Europe.
The Nazi plan for the genocide of all Jews in Europe, implemented through extermination camps from 1941 to 1945.
Antisemitic and racist laws enacted in 1935 that stripped German Jews of citizenship and imposed severe restrictions on their lives.
The strategy of lightning war used by Germany, combining air power, tanks, and motorised infantry for rapid breakthroughs.
The Allied invasion of Nazi-occupied France on 6 June 1944, the largest seaborne invasion in history.
The top-secret American research project that produced the first nuclear weapons during World War II.
The February 1945 wartime meeting of US, UK, and Soviet leaders to plan post-war reorganisation of Europe.
The August 1945 agreement between the Allied powers on the treatment of defeated Germany and the post-war order.
An intergovernmental organisation founded in 1945 to promote international peace, security, and cooperation.
The process by which colonies became independent of the colonising country, affecting dozens of nations between 1945 and 1975.
A policy by which a country avoids alignment with any major power bloc during the Cold War.
A conflict between North Korea (supported by China and the USSR) and South Korea (supported by the United States and UN) that ended in an armistice.
A prolonged conflict in which the United States supported South Vietnam against the communist North, becoming a defining moment of American Cold War policy.
A 13-day confrontation between the United States and Soviet Union over Soviet ballistic missiles in Cuba, the closest the Cold War came to nuclear war.
A barrier constructed by East Germany in 1961 to prevent emigration to West Germany, becoming the most potent symbol of the Cold War.
The 20th-century competition between the United States and Soviet Union for supremacy in spaceflight capability.
The first artificial Earth satellite, launched by the Soviet Union on 4 October 1957, igniting the Space Race.
The US spaceflight programme that landed twelve astronauts on the Moon between 1969 and 1972.
The Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, a cartel of oil-producing nations that exerts significant influence on global oil prices.
The General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, a multilateral treaty signed in 1947 to promote international trade by reducing tariffs.
An international financial institution providing loans and grants to developing countries for capital programmes.
The International Monetary Fund, an organisation working to foster global monetary cooperation and financial stability.
The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation, a political and military alliance of Western nations established in 1949.
A collective defence treaty among the Soviet Union and seven satellite states of Eastern Europe, established in 1955 to counter NATO.
The political reform in the Soviet Union after Stalin's death in 1953, involving a dismantling of the Stalinist system under Khrushchev.
A period of political liberalisation and mass protest in Czechoslovakia beginning in January 1968, crushed by Warsaw Pact invasion.
A sociopolitical purge campaign in China launched by Mao Zedong, targeting traditional elements and political enemies.
Mass protests centred in Beijing's Tiananmen Square in 1989, demanding political reform, which were suppressed by the Chinese government.
The process by which businesses and other organisations develop international influence or start operating on an international scale.
Economic liberalism based on deregulation, privatisation, and reduced government intervention, influential in Western economic policy since the 1980s.
A social and political movement addressing environmental issues like pollution, climate change, and biodiversity loss.
The system of institutionalised racial segregation and discrimination enforced by the National Party government of South Africa from 1948 to 1994.
The African National Congress, the political party that led the anti-apartheid movement and has governed South Africa since Nelson Mandela's election in 1994.
The South African anti-apartheid leader who became the country's first democratically elected president in 1994 after 27 years of political imprisonment.
A mass social movement in the United States that fought for racial equality and the end of institutionalised racial discrimination.
A non-violent protest in which demonstrators occupy seats or workspace to prevent business as usual, used extensively in the Civil Rights Movement.
Civil rights activists who rode interstate buses into the segregated southern United States in 1961 to challenge non-enforcement of Supreme Court rulings.
A political rally in Washington DC attended by 250,000 people to advocate for civil and economic rights for Black Americans, featuring Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech.
US federal legislation that prohibited discriminatory voting practices that had been responsible for widespread disenfranchisement of African Americans.
The series of political campaigns for reforms on issues such as women's suffrage, equal pay, reproductive rights, and an end to domestic violence.
A woman who campaigned for the right to vote in political elections, particularly members of the Women's Social and Political Union in Britain.
A period of radical political and societal change in France that began with the Estates General in 1789 and ended with Napoleon's coup in 1799.
A device for carrying out executions by decapitation, used extensively during the Reign of Terror in the French Revolution.
The period from September 1793 to July 1794 during which the French Revolutionary government executed thousands of perceived enemies.
The most radical political group of the French Revolution, led by Maximilien Robespierre, responsible for the Reign of Terror.
The period following the fall of Robespierre in July 1794 during which the revolutionary fervour was moderated.
The system of government in France from 1795 to 1799, preceding Napoleon's coup.
French military leader who rose to prominence during the French Revolution, eventually becoming Emperor of France and conquering much of Europe.
The battle in which Napoleon was finally defeated by the Duke of Wellington and Prussian forces, ending the Napoleonic Wars.
A series of agreements by the major powers of Europe to maintain the balance of power and suppress revolutions after the Napoleonic Wars.
A conference of ambassadors of European states that reshaped the continent after the Napoleonic Wars.
A series of political upheavals throughout Europe in 1848, driven by nationalist, liberal, and democratic ideals.
The process by which the German states united into a single state under Prussian leadership following the Franco-Prussian War.
The political and social movement that consolidated different states of the Italian peninsula into a single state, largely completed in 1861.
The political and social movement that resulted in the consolidation of different states of the Italian Peninsula into the Kingdom of Italy.
The Italian statesman who was the prime architect of Italian unification as the first prime minister of unified Italy.
An Italian general and nationalist who played a central role in the Risorgimento, leading the Expedition of the Thousand to conquer southern Italy.
The German statesman who masterminded the unification of Germany in 1871 and served as its first Chancellor.
A system of politics or principles based on practical rather than moral or ideological considerations, associated with Bismarck.
The colonisation and economic exploitation of Africa by European powers, accelerating during the 'Scramble for Africa' from 1881 to 1914.
The 1884–85 meeting of major European powers to regulate colonial competition and facilitate the colonisation of Africa.
A conflict between the British Empire and the two Boer republics (Transvaal and Orange Free State) over British suzerainty.
The 1952–1964 rebellion by the Kikuyu people of Kenya against British colonial rule, leading to independence in 1963.
Egypt's nationalisation of the Suez Canal, followed by a military invasion by Britain, France, and Israel, marking a turning point in the decline of European colonial power.
A conflict between France and Algerian independence movements, resulting in Algerian independence in 1962 after over one million deaths.
The war between France and the Viet Minh, ending in French defeat at Dien Bien Phu and the partition of Vietnam.
The Vietnamese communist revolutionary and statesman who served as Prime Minister of North Vietnam and drove the fight against French and US intervention.
The theory that if one country in a region fell to communism, neighbouring countries would follow, used to justify US intervention in Vietnam.
The US Congress's resolution that gave President Lyndon B. Johnson authorisation to escalate military involvement in Vietnam.
A series of surprise military attacks launched by the Viet Cong on South Vietnamese cities and towns during the lunar new year, turning American public opinion against the war.
The principle articulated in 1969 that the United States would assist allies but not directly fight their wars.
The easing of Cold War tensions, especially between the United States and Soviet Union during the 1970s under Nixon and Kissinger.
The Strategic Arms Limitation Talks, agreements between the United States and Soviet Union to limit nuclear weapons.
Agreements signed by 35 nations including the USA and USSR recognising European borders and committing to human rights improvements.
The revolution that replaced the Shah's monarchy with an Islamic Republic under Ayatollah Khomeini.
A nine-year conflict in which Soviet forces supported the communist government of Afghanistan against mujahideen insurgents.
The US strategy of supporting anti-communist movements around the world during the Ronald Reagan administration.
An eight-year conflict between Iran and Iraq that resulted in nearly one million casualties and no territorial changes.
The US-led coalition war to expel Iraqi forces from Kuwait following Saddam Hussein's invasion in August 1990.
Agreements signed between Israel and the Palestine Liberation Organisation establishing a framework for peace in the Middle East.
The mass slaughter of Tutsi people in Rwanda over 100 days in 1994, killing between 500,000 and 800,000 people while the world largely failed to intervene.
A series of ethnic conflicts and wars of independence in the former Yugoslavia following its dissolution.
The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks on the United States by al-Qaeda, which triggered the War on Terror and reshaped global geopolitics.
The international military campaign launched by the United States following the September 11 attacks, targeting terrorist organisations and states that support them.
A wave of pro-democracy protests and uprisings that took place in North Africa and the Middle East from 2010, leading to the fall of several governments.
A militant group that declared a 'caliphate' in parts of Iraq and Syria in 2014, carrying out mass atrocities before being largely defeated by 2019.
Long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns, mainly human-caused since the 20th century, now among the greatest challenges facing humanity.
An international treaty on climate change adopted in 2015, committing signatories to limit global warming to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels.
A global pandemic caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus, resulting in millions of deaths and massive economic disruption worldwide.
One of the world's first great civilisations, centred along the Nile River and known for pyramids, hieroglyphics, and a complex religious system.
The ruler of ancient Egypt, considered both a living god and a political sovereign with absolute power over the land.
Monumental stone structures built as tombs for Egyptian pharaohs, the most famous being the Great Pyramid of Giza built around 2560 BCE.
The process of preserving a dead body with chemicals, wrappings, and ritual, practised by the ancient Egyptians to prepare the deceased for the afterlife.
The ancient region between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, considered the cradle of civilisation and home to Sumer, Babylon, and Assyria.
One of the oldest deciphered writings of significant length, a Babylonian law code from about 1754 BCE.
King of Macedon who conquered the Persian Empire and large parts of Asia, creating one of the largest empires in ancient history.
The spread of Greek culture, language, and philosophy throughout the ancient world, particularly following Alexander the Great's conquests.
The form of government of Rome from 509 to 27 BCE, featuring a senate, consuls, and a complex system of checks and balances.
The river crossed by Julius Caesar with his army in 49 BCE, starting the Roman Civil War; 'crossing the Rubicon' now means taking an irreversible step.
The long period of relative peace and minimal expansion by the Roman Empire from 27 BCE to 180 CE under Augustus and his successors.
The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 CE, traditionally marking the end of ancient history and the beginning of the Middle Ages.
The eastern continuation of the Roman Empire, centred on Constantinople, which survived until 1453 when it fell to the Ottoman Turks.
A vast empire that lasted from 1299 to 1922 and at its height controlled much of Southeast Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa.
The longest-reigning Sultan of the Ottoman Empire, who presided over its peak in terms of military and economic power.
An empire in the Indian subcontinent that at its height controlled most of the Indian subcontinent and was known for religious tolerance and magnificent architecture.
The third Mughal Emperor, who expanded the empire to its greatest extent and promoted religious tolerance.
The largest contiguous land empire in history, founded by Genghis Khan in 1206 and stretching from Eastern Europe to the Sea of Japan.
The founder and Great Khan of the Mongol Empire, who united the nomadic tribes of Northeast Asia and conquered vast areas of Central Asia and China.
The devastating pandemic of bubonic plague that struck Europe in 1347–1351, killing approximately one-third of the continent's population.
A hierarchical system of landholding and political power in medieval Europe, based on loyalty between lord and vassal in exchange for land and protection.
A series of religious wars called by popes, aimed at recovering the Holy Land from Muslim control.
A powerful Catholic military order founded in 1119, originally to protect Christian pilgrims to the Holy Land.
A series of armed conflicts between England and France over succession to the French throne, lasting approximately 116 years.
A French peasant girl who, claiming divine guidance, led the French army to several important victories during the Hundred Years' War.
Gutenberg's invention around 1440 that revolutionised the production of books, enabling the spread of knowledge and the Protestant Reformation.
The period in European history when overseas exploration became a powerful factor in European culture and was the beginning of globalisation.
The Portuguese explorer who discovered the sea route to India around the Cape of Good Hope in 1498.
The Italian explorer who completed four voyages across the Atlantic Ocean for Spain, beginning the permanent European colonisation of the Americas.
The agreement between Spain and Portugal that divided the newly discovered lands outside Europe between the two colonial powers.
The 16th-century religious and political upheaval that fractured the Catholic Church and gave rise to Protestantism.
German theologian whose 1517 Ninety-Five Theses challenged papal authority and sparked the Protestant Reformation.
The Catholic Church's response to the Protestant Reformation, including internal reforms and the Council of Trent.
A conflict primarily fought in Central Europe, involving most of the major European continental powers, ending with the Peace of Westphalia.
The pair of peace treaties signed in 1648 ending the Thirty Years' War, establishing the modern concept of state sovereignty.
A period of new ideas in physics, astronomy, biology, medicine, and chemistry that transformed views of nature and society.
The paradigm shift from the Earth-centred to the Sun-centred model of the universe, associated with Nicolaus Copernicus.
Italian astronomer and physicist whose championing of heliocentrism led to his condemnation by the Catholic Church.
The three laws formulated by Isaac Newton that describe the relationship between a body and the forces acting upon it, foundational to classical mechanics.
Another name for the Enlightenment, an intellectual movement emphasising reason and individualism rather than tradition.
The theory that individuals consent to give up some freedoms in exchange for government protection of their remaining rights.
English philosopher whose theories on government, natural rights, and the social contract influenced the American and French Revolutions.
The political upheaval by which thirteen American colonies rejected British sovereignty and founded the United States of America.
The statement adopted by the Continental Congress on July 4, 1776 declaring the thirteen American colonies free from British rule.
The supreme law of the United States, establishing the framework of government and the rights of citizens.
The first ten amendments to the US Constitution, guaranteeing essential rights including freedom of speech, religion, and trial by jury.
The acquisition of the Louisiana Territory from France by the United States under President Thomas Jefferson for approximately $15 million.
The US foreign policy principle that the Western hemisphere was closed to further European colonisation.
The conflict between the Republic of Mexico and Texas colonists, resulting in the independence of the Republic of Texas.
The armed conflict between the United States and Mexico following US annexation of Texas, resulting in Mexico ceding vast territories to the USA.
The conflict between the Union (Northern states) and the Confederacy (Southern states) over slavery and states' rights.
The executive order issued by President Abraham Lincoln declaring enslaved people in Confederate states to be free.
The period following the Civil War during which the United States struggled to reintegrate the Confederate states and grant rights to freedmen.
Wealthy American industrialists who became enormously wealthy through questionable business practices in the late 19th century.
The use of antitrust law to break up monopolistic business trusts, particularly associated with President Theodore Roosevelt.
The first women's rights convention, held in Seneca Falls, New York, which produced the Declaration of Sentiments demanding equal rights for women.
A network of secret routes and safe houses used by enslaved African Americans to escape to free states and Canada.
The movement to end slavery, succeeding in Britain in 1833 and the United States with the 13th Amendment in 1865.
US legislation providing 160 acres of public land to settlers who agreed to farm and improve the land.
The first railroad line to connect the eastern and western United States, completed at Promontory Summit, Utah in 1869.
The transition from hand production methods to machine manufacturing that began in England around 1760 and spread worldwide.
The Watt steam engine, perfected in the 1770s, that powered factories, locomotives, and ships and drove the Industrial Revolution.
English textile workers of the early 19th century who protested against the introduction of labour-saving machinery by destroying it.
Organisations of workers formed to protect and further their rights and interests, becoming legal in Britain in 1871.
A working-class movement in Britain from 1838 to 1857 that demanded political reforms including universal male suffrage.
Statutes that maintained tariffs on imported grain to protect domestic agricultural interests, repealed in 1846.
The catastrophic mass starvation, disease, and emigration that occurred in Ireland between 1845 and 1852, caused by a potato blight.
The period of Queen Victoria's reign, characterised by industrialisation, imperial expansion, scientific progress, and strict social codes.
The first World's Fair, hosted in the Crystal Palace in London in 1851, showcasing industrial and artistic achievements from around the world.
The rapid invasion and colonisation of Africa by European powers, with European nations controlling 90% of the continent by 1914.
A powerful African kingdom in present-day South Africa that fought the British in the Anglo-Zulu War of 1879.
A conflict between the British Empire and the Zulu Kingdom, notable for both British defeats and eventual conquest.
Two wars between the British Empire and the Boer republics, resulting in British control of South Africa.
The coastal region of East Africa known for Swahili city-states that were major trading hubs connecting Africa, Arabia, and India.
The forced transportation of enslaved Africans across the Atlantic to the Americas, one of the largest forced migrations in history.
The Slave Trade Act 1807 that banned the slave trade in the British Empire, though slavery itself was not abolished until 1833.
The successful anti-slavery and anti-colonial revolt in the French colony of Saint-Domingue, leading to the founding of Haiti, the first Black republic.
The leader of the Haitian Revolution who transformed it from a slave revolt into a successful independence movement.
The Venezuelan military and political leader who played a key role in the independence movements of Venezuela, Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Panama.
Argentine general who led independence movements in Argentina, Chile, and Peru and was a key figure in South American liberation.
The US foreign policy principle that the Western Hemisphere was closed to further European colonisation, and that intervention by European powers would be considered an act of war.
The artificial waterway connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans through Panama, completed by the United States in 1914.
A conflict between the United States and Spain that ended Spanish colonial rule in the Americas and resulted in US acquisition of Cuba, Puerto Rico, and the Philippines.
A type of journalism that presents little or no legitimate well-researched news while using eye-catching headlines for increased sales, associated with the lead-up to the Spanish-American War.
The incorporation of Hawaii as a US territory in 1898, following the overthrow of the Hawaiian monarchy with American support.
The US foreign policy that called for equal trading rights for all nations in China and preservation of Chinese territorial integrity.
A military conflict between Russia and Japan over Korea and Manchuria, resulting in Japan's surprising victory.
The treaty mediated by US President Theodore Roosevelt ending the Russo-Japanese War, for which he won the Nobel Peace Prize.
The series of agreements between France and Britain settling colonial disputes and beginning their military alliance.
The diplomatic crisis that followed the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and escalated into World War I.
A military strategy aimed at wearing down the enemy through continuous losses of personnel and material, central to WWI tactics.
One of the largest battles of WWI, lasting from July to November 1916, with over one million casualties on both sides.
The Allied campaign to secure sea routes to Russia through the Dardanelles that ended in failure, particularly remembered for the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps.
The Australian and New Zealand Army Corps, soldiers from both nations who fought alongside British forces, particularly at Gallipoli in 1915.
US President who led America into WWI and proposed the Fourteen Points as the basis for a just peace.
Wilson's plan for peace after WWI that called for self-determination, the League of Nations, and open diplomacy.
The intergovernmental organisation founded as a result of the Paris Peace Conference following WWI, the forerunner to the United Nations.

A letter from the British Foreign Secretary declaring British support for a Jewish homeland in Palestine, with the provision that civil and religious rights of non-Jewish communities would be protected.
A secret treaty between Britain and France dividing the Arab provinces of the Ottoman Empire into spheres of influence, shaping the modern Middle East.
The systematic mass extermination of approximately 1.5 million Armenians by the Ottoman Empire during World War I.
The catastrophic influenza pandemic that killed between 50 and 100 million people worldwide, more than World War I itself.
The decade of economic growth and widespread prosperity in the United States and Western Europe, characterised by a distinctive cultural edge.
The stock market crash of October 1929 that began the Great Depression, wiping out millions of investors and triggering a global economic crisis.
A period of severe dust storms that greatly damaged the ecology and agriculture of the American and Canadian prairies during the Great Depression.
The series of programs introduced by President Roosevelt to provide relief, recovery, and reform during the Great Depression.
A coalition of left-wing parties uniting against fascism in France and Spain during the 1930s.
The conflict between the elected Republican government and the fascist Nationalist movement led by General Franco.
The annexation of Austria by Nazi Germany in March 1938.
The agreement by which Britain and France allowed Germany to annex the Sudetenland in exchange for peace, a defining act of appeasement.
The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union, secretly dividing Eastern Europe between them.
The program under which the United States supplied Allied nations with food, oil, and war materials from 1941 to 1945.
The surprise military attack by Japan on the US naval base at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii on December 7, 1941, drawing the United States into WWII.
One of the largest battles of WWII, in which the Soviet Union decisively defeated Germany on the Eastern Front.
The Japanese city on which the United States dropped the first atomic bomb on August 6, 1945, killing approximately 70,000–80,000 people immediately.
A series of military tribunals held after WWII to prosecute prominent members of the Nazi leadership for war crimes and crimes against humanity.
The US policy to support free peoples who were resisting attempted subjugation by armed minorities or outside pressures.
The Western Allies' response to the Soviet blockade of West Berlin, supplying the city entirely by air for 11 months.
The establishment of Israel as an independent state on May 14, 1948, following the end of the British Mandate for Palestine.
The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation, a political and military alliance of 30 countries committed to collective defence.
The victory of the Communist Party of China under Mao Zedong over the Nationalist government, founding the People's Republic of China.
The conflict between North Korea (aided by China) and South Korea (aided by the US and UN) that ended with an armistice maintaining the 38th parallel boundary.
The practice of making accusations of subversion or treason without proper evidence, associated with Senator Joseph McCarthy's anti-communist crusade.
The Supreme Court ruling that declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional.
A nationwide revolt against the Soviet-backed government of Hungary, crushed by Soviet military intervention.
The reaction in the Western world to the Soviet launch of Sputnik in 1957, triggering increased science education and the Space Race.
The failed CIA-sponsored invasion of Cuba by Cuban exiles, a significant embarrassment for President Kennedy.
The barrier constructed by East Germany on August 13, 1961, to prevent emigration to the West; its fall on November 9, 1989 symbolised the end of the Cold War.
The achievement of Apollo 11 on July 20, 1969, when American astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first humans to walk on the Moon.
The political scandal arising from a break-in at the Watergate complex and the Nixon administration's attempts at cover-up, leading to Nixon's resignation.
A framework for peace between Egypt and Israel brokered by President Carter, the first peace agreement between Israel and an Arab nation.
The opening of the Berlin Wall on November 9, 1989, symbolising the end of the Cold War division of Europe.
The formal breakup of the USSR on December 25, 1991, which ended the Cold War and brought independence to 15 new nations.
The series of agreements between Israel and the Palestine Liberation Organisation attempting to achieve a two-state solution.
The political and economic union of 27 European member states founded by the Maastricht Treaty in 1993, promoting peace, democracy, and shared prosperity.
